# Objective and Context

Each team will build a [*simple Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) notebook*]{.uublue-bold} over a [*small, domain-scoped SEC 10-K corpus*]{.uublue-bold}. This is [*not*]{.uublue-bold} yet the full enterprise RAG system. Instead, this project is a [*controlled prototype*]{.uublue-bold} that helps your team learn how a retrieval pipeline works before you build the larger system in Project 3.

Your team will begin from the [*smaller extracted subset*]{.uublue-bold} created in the previous step. Rather than indexing the full 10-K filing, you will work with a focused dataset such as:

- [*AI investment / AI strategy*]{.uublue-bold}
- [*Human capital / workforce*]{.uublue-bold}
- [*Data center / infrastructure*]{.uublue-bold}
- another approved narrow domain extracted from the filing

The goal is to build a notebook that can:

* embed the selected document chunks
* store them in a vector index
* retrieve relevant chunks for a question
* generate an answer using an LLM
* evaluate the quality of the retrieval and response

You can use the Following 3 notebooks (already shared with you) as references for how to build this prototype:

- [Simple RAG](https://drive.google.com/file/d/16UeBOPyVHFv1sdhioOR2-KiZSJ87fpBO/view?usp=drive_link)
- [Simple RAG With Reranking](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1xTfyvqNxej_C6nXZvYiVsZI2zSwIyv1X/view?usp=drive_link)
- [RAG with LangSmith](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1uwQz7JjOUU2xvvXQfKNIht9xO2v6qeRx/view?usp=drive_link)


:::{.callout-important}
This project is intentionally scoped to a [*notebook interface in Google Colab*]{.uublue-bold}. A chatbot interface is [*not required*]{.uublue-bold}. You are being asked to understand the mechanics of a RAG pipeline over a well-scoped financial document subset. The quality of your retrieval logic, grounding discipline, and evaluation matters more than interface complexity.
:::

# Key Takeaways

By completing this project, your team should understand:

- A RAG pipeline works end-to-end
- [*small domain-scoped corpora*]{.uublue-bold} often perform better than unfiltered full-document corpora
- [*Embeddings*]{.uublue-bold} influence retrieval quality
- [*Compare local/open models and API-based models*]{.uublue-bold}
- [*Evaluate*]{.uublue-bold} whether an answer is actually grounded in retrieved evidence


# Project Scope

## What You Will Build

You will build a [*simple notebook-based RAG pipeline*]{.uublue-bold} that works over a [*small extracted SEC 10-K dataset*]{.uublue-bold}. Your notebook should include:

* loading the extracted domain dataset
* chunk inspection
* embedding generation
* vector storage / similarity search
* retrieval of top-k chunks
* answer generation with a selected LLM
* evaluation of retrieval and grounded response quality


# Required Data Input

Your team must use the [*smaller filtered dataset produced earlier*]{.uublue-bold}, not the full filing. This can be exracted from any sec 10-K HTML File.

- AI-related extracted text from [*Item 1, Item 1A, Item 7*]{.uublue-bold}
- human capital extracted text from [*Item 1, Item 11*]{.uublue-bold}
- data center / infrastructure extracted text from [*Item 1, Item 7*]{.uublue-bold}

You should already have:

* cleaned text
* chunked text
* metadata such as:

  * `chunk_id`
  * `company_name`
  * `cik`
  * `section_id`
  * `token_count`

If you do not yet have chunked data, you may create it at the start of this project using your Assignment 2 pipeline.

You can use the following startup code.

## Connecting Google Drive to Colab

All the data is available in shared [google drive folder](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1tqqoqDfcOpGKBoRN7PpAtElcmzKcrbGz&usp=drive_fs). You can connect to it using the following code:

::: {#a06ffacf .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
```
:::


## Defining the Data Path

::: {#efdc1458 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from pathlib import Path

INPUT_DIR = Path("/content/drive/MyDrive/SEC-10K-2024-HTML")
OUTPUT_DIR = Path("/content/drive/MyDrive/SEC-10K-2024-ITEMS")

OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

html_files = sorted(INPUT_DIR.glob("*.html"))
len(html_files), html_files[:5]
```
:::


## Read Each Filing

::: {#5170cf1f .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def read_html_file(file_path):
    with open(file_path, "r", encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore") as f:
        return f.read()
```
:::


## Extract Specific SEC Items

You can use the starter function from assignment 2 or from here

::: {#4f6a67f9 .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import re

def normalize_text_for_matching(text):
    text = re.sub(r'\xa0', ' ', text)
    text = re.sub(r' ', ' ', text)
    text = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', text)
    return text

def extract_item_section(html_text, item_label, next_item_labels):
    """
    Extract a section beginning at item_label and ending before the first match
    of any label in next_item_labels.
    """
    text = normalize_text_for_matching(html_text)

    start_pattern = re.compile(rf'item\s*{re.escape(item_label)}[\.\:\-\s]', re.IGNORECASE)
    start_match = start_pattern.search(text)

    if not start_match:
        return None

    start_idx = start_match.start()

    end_idx = len(text)
    for next_label in next_item_labels:
        next_pattern = re.compile(rf'item\s*{re.escape(next_label)}[\.\:\-\s]', re.IGNORECASE)
        next_match = next_pattern.search(text, pos=start_match.end())
        if next_match:
            end_idx = min(end_idx, next_match.start())

    return text[start_idx:end_idx].strip()
```
:::


##  Define Item Extraction Map

::: {#bb21954e .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
ITEM_BOUNDARIES = {
    "1": ["1A", "1B", "2"],
    "1A": ["1B", "2", "3"],
    "3": ["4", "5"],
    "7": ["7A", "8", "9"],
    "7A": ["8", "9"],
    "10": ["11", "12", "13"],
    "11": ["12", "13", "14"],
    "12": ["13", "14", "15"],
    "13": ["14", "15"],
}
```
:::


Choose only the items relevant to your domain.

::: {#5b497be6 .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
SELECTED_ITEMS = ["1", "1A", "7"]   # Example for AI strategy domain
```
:::


## Save Extracted Item Files

This is the part you wanted: save each extracted item with a filename like:

currentfilename_item_1.html
currentfilename_item_1A.html
currentfilename_item_7.html


You can also choose to save this in a json or an excel file, hwever that copudl become very large and hard to manage, so I suggest saving each item as a separate html file. You can also save the metadata in a separate csv or json file if needed.

::: {#b2bea53e .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def save_extracted_items(file_path, selected_items, output_dir):
    html_text = read_html_file(file_path)
    base_name = file_path.stem

    saved_files = []

    for item in selected_items:
        next_items = ITEM_BOUNDARIES.get(item, [])
        extracted = extract_item_section(html_text, item, next_items)

        if extracted and len(extracted) > 500:  # avoid saving tiny false matches
            out_file = output_dir / f"{base_name}_item_{item}.html"
            with open(out_file, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
                f.write(extracted)
            saved_files.append(str(out_file))

    return saved_files

Run over all filings:

all_saved = []

for file_path in html_files:
    saved = save_extracted_items(file_path, SELECTED_ITEMS, OUTPUT_DIR)
    all_saved.extend(saved)

print(f"Saved {len(all_saved)} extracted item files.")
all_saved[:10]
```
:::


# Platform and Model Options

You may choose [*one*]{.uublue-bold} of the following generation approaches, You may run these using Hugging Face Transformers, sentence-transformers, or another approved inference method compatible with Colab:

- API-based models (preffered)
    - OpenAI
    - Gemini
    - Claude
- Local/open models from Hugging Face
    - Mistral
    - BAAI
    - Qwen


## Embedding Options

You may choose embedding models:

 - sentence-transformers models
 - BAAI embedding models
 - OpenAI embeddings
 - Gemini-compatible embedding flow if available

## Your team must clearly state:

* which embedding model was used
* which generation model was used
* why that choice was appropriate for your domain and compute budget

# Overall Pipeline

```{dot}
digraph RAGPipeline {
    rankdir=LR;
    node [shape=box, style=rounded];

    A [label="Filtered SEC 10-K Domain Corpus"];
    B [label="Chunked Documents"];
    C [label="Embedding Model"];
    D [label="Vector Index"];
    E [label="User Question"];
    F [label="Top-k Retrieval"];
    G [label="Context Assembly"];
    H [label="LLM Generation"];
    I [label="Grounded Answer + Evaluation"];

    A -> B -> C -> D;
    E -> F;
    D -> F -> G -> H -> I;
}
```



# Create the Project Notebook

Create a notebook in your project folder.

- `project2_rag_notebook.ipynb`
  - code
  - outputs
  - explanation
  - interpretation

# Load the Small Extracted Domain Corpus

Load the filtered dataset from the previous step.

* `ai_filtered_chunks.csv`
* `human_capital_chunks.parquet`
* `datacenter_chunks.csv`

Your notebook should briefly describe:

* what domain was selected
* which SEC Items were used
* how the smaller subset was produced
* why this scope is appropriate for a simple RAG prototype


# Inspect the Corpus Before Embedding

Before embedding, inspect the data. At minimum, show:

* number of chunks
* sections included
* average token length
* sample chunks

Discuss:

* whether the chunks look coherent
* whether the extraction seems too broad or too narrow
* whether your domain terms are well represented


# Build the Embedding Layer

Generate embeddings for your chunked documents. Your notebook must show:

* embedding model selected
* how the embeddings are created
* the dimension of the embedding vectors
* the shape of the resulting embedding matrix

Explain:

* why this embedding model was chosen
* whether it is local/open or API-based
* any compute trade-offs

# Build the Vector Store / Retrieval Index

Store the embeddings in a retrieval structure such as:

* FAISS
* Chroma

You must demonstrate:

* that the index was created successfully
* that queries can retrieve relevant chunks
* the value of `top_k` you selected

You should explain:

* why `top_k` was chosen
* what happens if retrieval returns too few or too many chunks


# Implement the Simple RAG Generation Step

Build a simple pipeline:

1. accept a user question
2. retrieve top-k chunks
3. assemble context
4. send that context to the LLM
5. generate an answer

This can be done with:

* plain Python functions
* LangChain
* a simplified RAG workflow inspired by the example notebooks

You do [*not*]{.uublue-bold} need a chatbot. A few notebook query cells are sufficient.

---

# Ask Structured Domain Questions

Your team must test the notebook with [*at least 8–10 questions*]{.uublue-bold} that fit the selected domain.

Examples for AI domain:

* Does the company describe AI as a strategic investment area?
* Is AI framed as operational efficiency, innovation, or risk?
* Are AI-related expenditures or R&D activities discussed?
* Does the company mention AI-related uncertainty or regulatory concerns?

Examples for human capital domain:

* Does the company identify labor shortages as a risk?
* Is workforce development described as a strategic priority?
* Are compensation or retention issues mentioned?

Examples for data center / infrastructure domain:

* Does the company discuss investment in data centers or cloud infrastructure?
* Is infrastructure linked to scaling digital services?
* Are data center or compute demands framed as cost drivers?

---

# Show Retrieval Outputs Explicitly

For each sample query, show:

* the question
* retrieved chunks
* chunk scores if available
* chunk metadata
* generated answer

# Evaluate the RAG System

Your notebook must include a simple evaluation section. You do [*not*]{.uublue-bold} need a perfect benchmark, but you must evaluate the system in a structured way.

## Minimum required evaluation dimensions

### Retrieval Relevance

For a small set of test questions:

* Were the retrieved chunks relevant?
* Did retrieval capture the right domain evidence?

This can be done manually using a small table.

### Grounding

Check whether the answer is actually supported by the retrieved chunks.

Questions to ask:

* Did the answer rely only on retrieved evidence?
* Did the answer introduce unsupported claims?

### Failure Analysis

Identify at least [*3 failure cases*]{.uublue-bold} such as:

* weak retrieval
* irrelevant chunk returned
* answer too vague
* answer hallucinated beyond retrieved context
* domain filtering too broad or too narrow

## Optional advanced evaluation

If your team wants, you may use ideas from the example notebooks such as:

* RAGAS-style evaluation
* faithfulness/relevance checks
* LangSmith-style tracing

These are optional enhancements, not required for baseline credit.

# Reflect on Design Trade-Offs
Your notebook must include a discussion of:


* why working on a small filtered corpus helped retrieval
* what limitations remain
* whether local/open models performed differently from API-based models
* how this notebook should evolve into a full system in Project 3


# Deliverables

Your submission must include:

## Required

* 1 notebook (`.ipynb` or `.qmd`)
* rendered HTML or PDF if applicable
* output showing retrieval and answer generation
* evaluation section
* references if external methods or models are discussed

## Optional

* GitHub repository
* GitHub Pages site
* additional experiment notebook
* extra evaluation module

GitHub is [*optional*]{.uublue-bold} for this project.


# Suggested Notebook Structure {.unnumbered}

```text
1. Title and project overview
2. Domain selection and SEC Item scope
3. Data loading and corpus inspection
4. Embedding setup
5. Vector index creation
6. Simple retrieval function
7. Simple generation function
8. Query experiments
9. Evaluation
10. Failure analysis
11. Reflection and next steps
```

# Recommended Coding Sequence {.unnumbered}

Students should proceed in this order:

1. verify the small filtered corpus
2. generate embeddings
3. create vector index
4. test retrieval only
5. inspect retrieved chunks manually
6. add generation step
7. test multiple questions
8. evaluate
9. document limitations

# Important Constraints

## You must

* use the smaller extracted domain corpus
* show retrieved chunks explicitly
* evaluate the notebook’s performance
* explain your design choices

## You may

* use APIs or local/open models
* use LangChain or plain Python
* use FAISS or another approved vector store
* use Colab secrets for API keys if needed

## You should not

* build a full chatbot
* index the entire raw 10-K corpus
* skip retrieval inspection
* treat generation quality as sufficient without checking grounding


# Submission Checklist

* [ ] selected one narrow SEC domain
* [ ] used the filtered chunk dataset from the previous step
* [ ] generated document embeddings
* [ ] created a vector retrieval index
* [ ] implemented a simple RAG pipeline
* [ ] tested at least 8–10 domain questions
* [ ] showed retrieved chunks and responses
* [ ] evaluated retrieval and grounding
* [ ] documented failures and limitations
